english: Bara Chirashi Spicy Salmon Scallop Ikura Don

food_subgroup: Japanese rice bowl (donburi) with mixed seafood toppings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Trio Don or mixed-seafood donburi. FOODON_00002572 (rice bowl food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FHV (Rice dish) is the closest available parent. No specific donburi or mixed-seafood rice bowl code exists in FoodEx2.

main_ingredients: spicy salmon (marinated raw salmon with chilli sauce)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a base of Japanese short-grain rice (~200 g) topped with a combination of bara chirashi, spicy salmon, scallops, and ikura salmon roe (~250 g combined toppings).

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: Japanese short-grain cooked rice (USDA FoodData Central #168878), raw mixed seafood sashimi composite, salmon roe (USDA FoodData Central #175168), and scallops raw (USDA FoodData Central #175177); spicy marinade contribution estimated from standard chilli-mayo sauce composition.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Trio Don is a restaurant-specific composite dish. No direct entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA for this exact combination. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 44% cooked Japanese rice, 20% mixed raw fish (tuna, salmon, yellowtail), 15% spicy salmon with chilli-mayo marinade, 12% raw scallops, and 9% ikura salmon roe. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; Japanese white rice GI reference (Sugiyama et al., 2003, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition); mixed seafood contribution assumed GI ~5 (protein/fat dominant, negligible glycemic contribution).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Trio Don. GI is estimated as a weighted blend: cooked Japanese short-grain rice has a published GI of approximately 68–72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Sugiyama et al. 2003). The seafood and roe toppings (~56% of dish by weight) are protein- and fat-dominant with negligible glycemic carbohydrates, effectively contributing GI ~0–5. Weighted composite GI estimated at approximately 54, placing the dish in the low GI category (≤55). Glycemic load per 450 g serving = (54 × 78.75 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 42.5 g. Note: GL is high due to large serving size despite moderate GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100g; ~1395 mg per 450 g serving) from soy-based marinades, seasoned rice, and ikura
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full serving of Trio Don may contribute approximately 1395 mg sodium, representing over 60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; portion reduction or requesting reduced-sodium marinade is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~42.5 g) driven by a large rice base, despite a moderate composite GI of approximately 54
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the composite GI of Trio Don is estimated at 54 (low category), the large serving size results in a high glycemic load of approximately 42.5 g per bowl; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion or sharing the dish to manage postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from salmon, scallops, and ikura; low saturated fat content (~0.8 g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Trio Don provides a favourable cardiovascular profile due to its high omega-3 polyunsaturated fat content from salmon and ikura, low saturated fat, and lean protein from seafood; however, sodium intake should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Trio Don is a Japanese-style rice bowl (donburi) dish served at The Oyster Bank restaurant in Singapore, combining three of the restaurant's signature toppings over a base of seasoned Japanese short-grain shari rice. The three components are: Bara Chirashi (a colourful assortment of diced raw fish including tuna, salmon, and yellowtail arranged in a scattered style), Spicy Salmon (fresh salmon marinated in a chilli-based sauce, typically incorporating chilli mayo or sriracha), and Scallops (raw or lightly seared sweet sea scallops). The entire bowl is finished with Ikura, which are glistening orange salmon roe pearls that add a briny, oceanic burst of flavour. The dish offers a complex interplay of textures — the soft, slightly sticky rice contrasts with the silky raw fish, the tender scallops, and the delicate pop of the ikura. Nutritionally, Trio Don is a protein-rich, omega-3-dense meal with moderate energy density (~148 kcal/100g) and a composite glycemic index estimated at approximately 54 (low GI category), though the glycemic load per full serving is high due to the substantial rice base. The dish is high in vitamin B12, selenium, and vitamin D from the seafood components. Clinically, it is a favourable choice for cardiovascular health due to its omega-3 content and low saturated fat, but sodium content from marinades and seasoned rice warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not halal-certified and contains fish, shellfish, and roe allergens.

confidence_reason: Trio Don is a restaurant-specific composite dish from The Oyster Bank, Singapore. No direct entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or any other approved primary source for this exact dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central data for individual ingredients (cooked Japanese rice, raw mixed fish, salmon roe, scallops). GI is estimated by analogous weighting from published GI values for Japanese short-grain rice (University of Sydney GI Database; Sugiyama et al. 2003). Confidence is medium due to the estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis using USDA FoodData Central approved sources. No direct laboratory analysis of this dish is available. Values should be treated as indicative estimates. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero by formulation as this dish contains no hydrogenated fats; all fats are from natural seafood and rice sources. 3. GI is estimated at 54 (low category, ≤55) using a weighted composite of Japanese short-grain rice GI (~68–72, University of Sydney GI Database) and seafood toppings (GI effectively 0–5 due to negligible glycemic carbohydrates). The composite GI of 54 is classified as low per the Veritas threshold (≤55 = low). 4. Glycemic load per 450 g serving is approximately 42.5 g, which is high, driven by the large rice base. 5. Sodium estimate (~310 mg/100g) includes contributions from soy-based marinades, seasoned shari rice, and ikura; actual sodium may vary by restaurant preparation. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and are not individually verified for this specific restaurant dish. 7. The cuisine tag 'Alcoholic Drink' in the input source data appears to be a field survey platform categorisation artefact and is not reflected in this record, as Trio Don is a food dish, not a beverage. 8. Halal status is not confirmed; The Oyster Bank is not listed as a halal-certified establishment.
